allegations.text: Air Canada's chatbot told a passenger he could request a bereavement discount retroactively within 90 days after travel. The answer conflicted with Air Canada's bereavement-fare terms, which required approval before travel. The tribunal treated the chatbot answer as part of Air Canada's customer-facing service rather than a separate legal actor.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Air Canada bereavement-fare policy
allegations.mitigation_gap: No effective guardrail prevented the chatbot from presenting a refund rule inconsistent with the canonical policy, and no escalation path corrected the answer before purchase.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Passenger relied on the chatbot answer, bought tickets, and obtained a partial damages award.

allegations.text: Counsel filed a brief containing nonexistent cases, false quotations, and false citations generated through ChatGPT. The lawyer then asked ChatGPT whether the cases were real and relied on the chatbot's answer instead of checking legal databases.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Federal reporters, legal research databases, and actual case law
allegations.mitigation_gap: No independent cite-checking against legal databases; AI self-verification was substituted for source verification; supervising attorney signed without adequate review.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Underlying lawsuit was dismissed and counsel/firm were sanctioned.

allegations.text: A reply brief cited a case that did not exist. The Second Circuit identified the citation as apparently generated by ChatGPT and referred counsel to the court's grievance panel.
allegations.deployer: Counsel for appellant in Second Circuit appeal
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Second Circuit case law and cited legal authority
allegations.mitigation_gap: No cite-checking caught the nonexistent case before filing; counsel did not ensure AI-assisted legal research matched real authority.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Counsel was referred for possible discipline and the court issued a published warning on AI verification duties.

allegations.text: Attorneys filed briefing containing nonexistent or unsupported case citations attributed to generative AI use. Reporting and case-analysis sources describe a sanctions response after the court identified fabricated authority.
allegations.ai_system_name: ChatGPT / generative AI legal drafting workflow
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual case law and court record
allegations.mitigation_gap: AI-generated legal citations were not verified against source materials before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Lawyers were fined and the firm faced sanctions-related scrutiny.

allegations.text: A reply brief contained fabricated quotations and additional misrepresentations. The Fifth Circuit found that AI-generated material had been filed without adequate verification and that counsel's response to the show-cause order compounded the problem.
allegations.ai_system_name: Generative AI, identified in reporting as Casetext/CoCounsel and/or vLex workflow
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Fifth Circuit record, quoted opinions, served Rule 11 materials
allegations.mitigation_gap: No independent verification of quotations or propositions before filing; show-cause response was evasive about AI authorship.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Attorney sanctioned and ordered to pay $2,500 to the clerk.

allegations.text: A post-hearing brief contained fictitious cases, false transcript citations, false Rule 4 file citations, and references to transcript pages that did not exist. Counsel admitted AI use in connection with the brief.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: ASBCA precedents, transcript volumes, and Rule 4 appeal file
allegations.mitigation_gap: Counsel and staff reviewed the brief but did not verify citations against the actual record or authorities; safeguards were found inadequate.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Board struck the entire reply brief and denied leave to refile.

allegations.text: Class counsel used multiple AI tools to draft and cross-check a class settlement approval brief. The filing contained fabricated quotations from real cases and at least one nonexistent case, and a corrected filing failed to remove the hallucinations.
allegations.ai_system_name: Six unspecified AI tools used concurrently
allegations.deployer: Plaintiff class counsel in Vuori wage-and-hour case
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual case law and settlement approval standards
allegations.mitigation_gap: AI tools were used to verify each other instead of checking legal databases; corrected filing did not correct false authority.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Settlement motions were struck; counsel was found inadequate as class counsel; class settlement collapsed into individual resolution.

allegations.text: Attorney filings contained fabricated AI-generated citations in a federal matter. The AI issue was part of a broader disciplinary record that included evasive responses and other noncompliance.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual case law and federal court filing obligations
allegations.mitigation_gap: No citation verification; failure to correct when challenged; broader noncompliance with court and bar processes.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Attorney was suspended in federal court and later disciplined by the Florida Supreme Court.

allegations.text: Application software automatically rejected female applicants age 55 or older and male applicants age 60 or older. The EEOC alleged the screening logic unlawfully denied older applicants an employment opportunity.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Age Discrimination in Employment Act
allegations.mitigation_gap: No pre-deployment age-discrimination audit or human override prevented hard-coded age thresholds from eliminating qualified applicants.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: More than 200 applicants were allegedly rejected; company settled with EEOC for $365,000 and injunctive relief.

allegations.text: Tenant-screening score allegedly relied heavily on credit history and non-tenancy debt in a way that disadvantaged Black and Hispanic rental applicants using housing vouchers. DOJ and HUD filed a statement of interest supporting Fair Housing Act disparate-impact applicability to algorithmic tenant screening.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Fair Housing Act and voucher-backed rent-payment facts
allegations.mitigation_gap: No adequate fair-housing disparate-impact testing; model allegedly omitted or undervalued voucher payment certainty.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Voucher holders allegedly denied housing; settlement created monetary and injunctive relief.

allegations.text: MiDAS issued automated unemployment-fraud determinations that were later found overwhelmingly erroneous. Claimants were assessed penalties and collection actions after automated determinations without adequate process.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Unemployment insurance fraud standards and constitutional due-process requirements
allegations.mitigation_gap: Automated flags lacked meaningful human review and adequate notice/opportunity to contest; error monitoring and escalation failed.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Thousands of claimants were wrongfully accused and faced garnishments, penalties, and collection actions; state settled for $20 million.

allegations.text: Arkansas replaced nurse assessment logic with a computerized RUGs allocation model that reduced home-care hours for many Medicaid beneficiaries. Litigation alleged the system failed to account for individualized conditions and was implemented without proper rulemaking or due process.
allegations.ai_system_name: Resource Utilization Groups (RUGs) computerized care-allocation algorithm
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Medicaid home- and community-based services rules, state APA procedures, individualized care needs
allegations.mitigation_gap: No adequate rulemaking, transparency, individualized validation, or notice process accompanied the algorithmic allocation change.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Disabled beneficiaries lost care hours and challenged the system; state-court rulings and later federal litigation led to relief and reforms.

allegations.text: FTC alleged Rite Aid deployed facial recognition in stores and generated false-positive matches that disproportionately affected certain consumers, including people of color. Employees allegedly acted on false alerts by surveilling, searching, removing, or reporting customers.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Accurate identity evidence and consumer-protection obligations
allegations.mitigation_gap: Insufficient testing, monitoring, bias controls, employee training, and consumer notice before acting on face-recognition alerts.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Consumers were allegedly wrongly accused, embarrassed, or subjected to law-enforcement contact; FTC order banned Rite Aid from using facial recognition for five years.

allegations.text: A grainy surveillance still was run through facial recognition and returned Robert Williams as a candidate. Police allegedly relied on the output and a flawed photo identification process, leading to a wrongful arrest for a theft he did not commit.
allegations.ai_system_name: Michigan State Police / DataWorks Plus facial-recognition pipeline
allegations.deployer: Detroit Police Department and Michigan State Police
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity and warrant probable-cause standards
allegations.mitigation_gap: No policy requiring independent corroboration before arrest; warrant materials did not adequately disclose the limits of the face-recognition lead.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Williams was arrested, detained, and later brought a civil-rights suit; settlement included damages and policy reforms.

allegations.text: A blurry fake-ID image was run through facial recognition and returned Nijeer Parks as a possible match. Police allegedly obtained a warrant without adequately verifying alibi evidence or disclosing the uncertainty of the technology.
allegations.ai_system_name: Clearview AI alleged / out-of-state facial-recognition lead
allegations.deployer: Woodbridge Police Department and intelligence-center intermediaries
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity, alibi evidence, and warrant probable-cause standards
allegations.mitigation_gap: No adequate alibi check, DNA/fingerprint corroboration, or transparent disclosure of FRT provenance before arrest.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Parks spent about 10 days in jail before charges were dismissed; civil-rights litigation followed.

allegations.text: Detroit police used a facial-recognition lead to place Porcha Woodruff in a lineup for a carjacking investigation. The suit alleges obvious exculpatory circumstances, including pregnancy, were not adequately reconciled before arrest.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity and warrant probable-cause standards
allegations.mitigation_gap: FRT output seeded the lineup without sufficient independent corroboration or review of distinguishing characteristics.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Woodruff was arrested while pregnant, detained, and later sued after charges were dropped.

allegations.text: A private retail loss-prevention workflow allegedly matched robbery footage to old mugshots of Harvey Murphy and communicated certainty to police. Murphy alleged the match and lineup process caused his arrest despite not being the robber.
allegations.deployer: EssilorLuxottica / Macy's loss-prevention personnel and Houston police
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Actual suspect identity and reliable biometric-comparison standards
allegations.mitigation_gap: Private FRT comparison lacked qualified-examiner controls; old photographs and employee pre-briefing allegedly contaminated the identification process; police allegedly did not independently investigate.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Murphy was detained and alleges severe jailhouse harm; civil case seeks damages.

allegations.text: Plaintiff alleges Workday's AI screening tools caused discriminatory job-application rejections based on age, race, and disability-related factors. The case is included as a vendor-liability candidate because the public record identifies AI screening and a court allowed key claims to proceed, but discrete error rows may need to be split by plaintiff or application event.
allegations.ai_system_name: Workday HiredScore / Candidate Skills Match tools
allegations.deployer: Workday, Inc. as HR software vendor
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: ADEA, Title VII, ADA, FEHA, and employer-specific applicant records
allegations.mitigation_gap: Alleged lack of disparate-impact audit, biased training/preferences, and absence of meaningful human review before rejection.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Plaintiff alleges repeated job rejections through Workday systems; ADEA collective certification was reported.

allegations.text: Plaintiff, a 24-year IBM veteran, alleges an algorithmic hiring/rehire screening tool blocked his rehire at age 48, constituting age discrimination. Filed as Swanson v. International Business Machines Corporation in the Western District of Texas on 2026-05-23. The precise technical nature of the tool (machine-learning vs. rules-based) is not yet established on the public record.
allegations.ai_system_name: Unnamed IBM algorithmic hiring/rehire screening tool
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: ADEA and applicant-specific rehire eligibility records
allegations.mitigation_gap: Alleged absence of meaningful human review of an automated rehire-eligibility screen and no disparate-impact safeguard against age-based exclusion.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Plaintiff alleges denial of rehire after 24 years of prior IBM employment, attributed to an automated screen.

allegations.text: CNN (a Warner Bros. Discovery subsidiary) sued Perplexity AI in the Southern District of New York (No. 1:26-cv-04427, filed 2026-05-28; jury demanded). The complaint alleges a two-stage harm: at the input stage, Perplexity’s "PerplexityBot" and "Perplexity-User" crawlers copy CNN content (over 17,000 works) to build an "AI-First" search index; at the output stage, Perplexity’s GenAI products (answer engine, enterprise chatbot, APIs, Comet browser) generate verbatim or substantially similar reproductions. CNN brings Copyright Act (17 U.S.C. § 101 et seq.) and Lanham Act claims, the latter including "confabulated or ‘hallucinated’ content that Perplexity falsely attributes to CNN."
allegations.ai_system_name: Perplexity answer engine + GenAI products (PerplexityBot / Perplexity-User crawlers; "AI-First" search index; RAG)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: CNN copyrighted works (17,000+ registrations) and CNN trademarks; 17 U.S.C. § 501 and the Lanham Act
allegations.mitigation_gap: Alleged crawling/copying of copyrighted content without licensing, and AI output that fabricates and falsely attributes content to CNN without adequate provenance or attribution controls.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: CNN alleges licensing/market harm from large-scale reproduction plus consumer confusion and brand dilution from hallucinated content falsely attributed to CNN.

allegations.text: In reciprocal attorney discipline, the Oklahoma Supreme Court publicly reprimanded attorney Matthew Brett Reeves (2026 OK 37, decided 2026-05-27) for filing five fabricated citations across two motions in the underlying federal matter Johnson v. Dunn, No. 2:21-cv-1701-AMM (N.D. Ala.). Reeves admitted the citations were "hallucinated by ChatGPT" and that he failed to verify them, violating his firm’s AI policy. The OBA noted this is the first reported Oklahoma attorney-discipline case involving AI-fabricated citations.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law and the duty of candor to the tribunal
allegations.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated citations against a real reporter before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated authorities submitted to a court; attorney received a public reprimand.

allegations.text: An Ohio trial court sanctioned pro se litigant Xingkui Guo $100 (2025-08-11) for citing five fabricated/mis-cited cases that the court found "appear to be the result of AI-generated hallucinations." On appeal, Guo admitted the mis-citations resulted from his use of the Chinese AI platform "Deepseek." The Sixth District Court of Appeals affirmed on 2026-05-26 (Guo v. Meade Motorcars, L.L.C., 2026-Ohio-1930), rejecting Guo’s motion to set aside the sanction.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine Ohio case law (cases cited did not exist at the given citations)
allegations.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated authorities before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated citations submitted on appeal; $100 monetary sanction imposed.

allegations.text: In a published per curiam opinion, the Sixth Circuit found that attorney Steven N. Howe used Westlaw CoCounsel to draft appellate briefs and filed them without verifying the authorities. The briefs contained three fabricated quotations attributed to real cases and misrepresented the holdings of United States v. Washington and United States v. Anthony. Howe admitted the AI-generated errors.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine holdings of the cited authorities and the duty of candor to the tribunal
allegations.mitigation_gap: Relied on non-attorney "staff" to supervise AI output; no verification of citations before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: False quotations and misleading legal arguments submitted in a criminal appeal; judicial resources expended; indigent defendant’s appeal delayed.

allegations.text: A magistrate judge sanctioned supervising attorney Lenden Webb after his firm filed a discovery letter brief containing a nonexistent case (South Pointe Wholesale v. Vilardi) and a misrepresented holding, "caused in part by use of AI." The associate had used Westlaw CoCounsel. Webb was admonished and personally sanctioned for failure to supervise and failure to obey court orders.
allegations.deployer: Webb Law Group, APC (Lenden Webb, supervising attorney)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law; duties of candor and supervision (Cal. Rules of Prof. Conduct 5.1)
allegations.mitigation_gap: No supervision of associate’s AI use; firm relied on a vendor representation that CoCounsel could not produce hallucinations.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Fake citation submitted to the court; judicial resources expended across multiple show-cause orders.

allegations.text: A district judge sanctioned plaintiff’s counsel Gavril T. Gabriel for citing a fabricated case ("In re Shubert, 799 F.3d 1124") three times across summary-judgment oppositions. Gabriel admitted using LexisNexis+ and its drafting feature Protégé. The court found bad faith, compounded by attempts to bury and minimize the misconduct.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law
allegations.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated citations; concealment of AI use until ordered to disclose.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated authority cited three times; bad-faith conduct found.

allegations.text: A magistrate judge sanctioned attorney Tricia S. Lindsay after she filed two opposition memoranda citing numerous nonexistent cases. By process of elimination (no human drafter claimed), the court found the citations "were generated as a result of the use of some kind of AI program," expressly declining to credit Lindsay’s belated suggestion that LexisNexis was the source. The court found subjective bad faith.
allegations.ai_system_name: Unspecified generative-AI program (court finding; attorney’s LexisNexis attribution not credited)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law; Rule 11 duty of reasonable inquiry
allegations.mitigation_gap: No citation verification; obfuscation about the source of the fabricated citations.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Numerous fabricated citations across multiple filings; referred (with co-counsel) to the Second Circuit Grievance Panel.

allegations.text: The Supreme Court of Alabama found that briefs filed by appellants’ counsel ("Hall") contained numerous invalid citations the court determined were "AI hallucinations," i.e., fake authorities created by an AI system. Counsel conceded the improper AI use, describing it as his first use of an AI research tool. The court dismissed the appeal as grossly deficient and imposed sanctions.
allegations.ai_system_name: Unnamed generative-AI research tool (court finding; counsel conceded)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine legal authorities; Alabama Rules of Appellate Procedure
allegations.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated authorities before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Fabricated authorities forced opposing party and the court to expend resources; appeal dismissed.

allegations.text: A district judge sanctioned defendants’ attorney Raja Rajan $5,000 under Rule 11(c) for his repeated improper use of generative AI in filings — his second such violation. The court rejected his plea for a lighter penalty, noting prior sanctions exceeding $73,000.
allegations.ai_system_name: Unnamed generative AI (ChatGPT referenced in order)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law; Rule 11 duty of inquiry
allegations.mitigation_gap: Failed to verify AI output despite a prior sanction and the court’s standing order on AI use.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Repeat submission of unverified AI-derived filings; escalating sanctions.

allegations.text: A district court struck a pro se plaintiff’s Rule 59(e) motion and sanctioned her $5,000 under Rule 11 after finding she "generated each brief using AI." The filings contained at least 13 hallucinated cases, quotations, and statements of law and fact.
allegations.deployer: Pro se plaintiff Ifeoma Delliane Chinedu Obi
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law; Rule 11
allegations.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated content; repeated violations after notice.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Multiple filings riddled with fabricated authority; motion struck.

allegations.text: An Illinois appellate court found that pro se appellant Robert S. Hulvat filed a brief "produced using artificial intelligence" that cited nine nonexistent cases and fictitious holdings, violating the Illinois Supreme Court AI policy and appellate rules. Hulvat asserted he had used a "well-known generative legal research tool."
allegations.ai_system_name: Unnamed generative legal-research tool (per appellant; court finding)
allegations.deployer: Pro se plaintiff-appellant Robert S. Hulvat
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine Illinois case law; Illinois Supreme Court Policy on AI; Rule 375
allegations.mitigation_gap: No verification of AI-generated authorities before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: Fictitious authorities forced opposing counsel to expend fees; dismissal affirmed.

allegations.text: A bankruptcy court sanctioned attorney Kathleen Wilson after she admitted using generative AI to prepare a Supplemental Response and other pleadings without verifying the output. The filings contained AI-generated citations to nonexistent cases, including In re Garcia and In re Buttermilk Towne LLC.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine case law; Fed. R. Bankr. P. 9011
allegations.mitigation_gap: Failed to review or verify admittedly AI-generated content before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: False citations submitted to the court; sanctions and mandatory AI education imposed.

allegations.text: The California Court of Appeal sanctioned respondent's appellate counsel Roxanne Chung Bonar $5,000 after she cited fictitious cases in a family-law appeal. At oral argument Bonar acknowledged she lacked a paid legal-research subscription and conceded the fictitious citations "may have" come from AI tools she was using. The same fake cases had also appeared in the family court's written order, which the appellate court found was error.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Non-existent California authorities (Twigg and Teegarden); genuine California family-law caselaw
allegations.mitigation_gap: Counsel used AI for legal research without a verified subscription and submitted citations without checking their existence.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $5,000 sanction payable to court clerk; referral to State Bar of California; family court order reversed for reliance on fictitious authority.

allegations.text: Attorney Kenneth A. Mayeaux used ChatGPT to draft a memorandum in support of a habeas petition and submitted it without verifying the citations. The memorandum contained hallucinated case law. Mayeaux admitted both using ChatGPT and failing to vet the citations before filing. The court sanctioned him $1,000 personally under Fed. R. Civ. P. 11 and 28 U.S.C. § 1927.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine federal immigration/habeas caselaw; Fed. R. Civ. P. 11
allegations.mitigation_gap: Attorney used ChatGPT to draft legal memoranda and filed without any citation verification.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $1,000 personal sanction against counsel; false citations submitted in habeas proceedings.

allegations.text: Drafting attorney Jalen R. Harris admitted starting a brief on Westlaw then "resorting to ChatGPT," submitting nine hallucinated case citations without checking them. The court imposed $250 on Harris and $1,000 on supervising attorney James Roquemore under Rule 11, and formally admonished co-counsel Corwin St. Raymond. Harris took full responsibility and acknowledged he never read the fabricated cases.
allegations.deployer: Jalen R. Harris; James Roquemore; Corwin St. Raymond
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Nine non-existent federal cases cited in the brief; genuine federal law
allegations.mitigation_gap: Drafting attorney used ChatGPT without verifying output; supervising attorney failed to catch the hallucinations.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $1,250 in monetary sanctions ($250 + $1,000); formal admonishment of co-counsel.

allegations.text: The Ohio Court of Appeals, Eleventh Appellate District, sanctioned an attorney after a non-attorney paralegal used ChatGPT to generate fabricated quotations that the attorney submitted in a criminal appeal. The attorney admitted the AI use and the court found the conduct sanctionable; it also noted the attorney had continued using ChatGPT after the sanctions hearing. Aggravating: a subsequent October 2025 filing visibly contained a ChatGPT prompt.
allegations.deployer: Respondent attorney (Ohio appellate counsel; name not stated in order)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Non-existent judicial quotations attributed to real Ohio judges; Ohio Rules of Professional Conduct
allegations.mitigation_gap: Attorney failed to review paralegal's ChatGPT output before submission; continued AI use after sanctions hearing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $2,000 monetary fine (credit for $2,000 already paid to Ashtabula County Prosecutor); referral to Ohio Office of Disciplinary Counsel; removal as counsel; application to reopen stricken; two-year order to serve judgment on falsely-implicated judges.

allegations.text: In a two-phase sanctions proceeding, the U.S. District Court for the District of Oregon struck plaintiff's summary-judgment briefs, dismissed claims with prejudice, and imposed sanctions on pro hac vice counsel Stephen Brigandi for submitting briefing containing non-existent cases and fabricated quotations that the court attributed to an unidentified AI tool. A December 2025 order imposed a $15,500 Rule 11 sanction; the March 2026 order finalized a fee award of $94,704.38 apportioned 85% to Brigandi and 15% to local counsel Timothy Murphy.
allegations.deployer: Stephen Brigandi (pro hac vice lead counsel)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Non-existent case citations and fabricated quotations; Fed. R. Civ. P. 11
allegations.mitigation_gap: Lead counsel submitted AI-generated briefing without verification; court found "one of the clients may have played a role in generating the fake citations" via an unidentified AI tool.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $15,500 monetary sanction on Brigandi; $94,704.38 attorney-fee award ($80,498.72 Brigandi, $14,205.66 Murphy); claims dismissed with prejudice; summary-judgment briefs stricken.

allegations.text: The Southern District of Alabama publicly reprimanded attorney Franklin Hollis Eaton, Jr. and imposed a $55,597 sanction after he submitted hundreds of pages of gibberish produced directly from Westlaw's CoCounsel AI service. Eaton denied using AI, but the court found his denial "incredible" because he had himself attached CoCounsel-generated documents. The order was directed published in the Federal Supplement and referred to the Alabama State Bar.
allegations.ai_system_name: CoCounsel (Westlaw) (named; court-found; counsel's denial not credited)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Accurate pleading standards; Court Local Rules; Fed. R. Civ. P. 11
allegations.mitigation_gap: Counsel attached raw, unreviewed CoCounsel output containing hundreds of pages of erroneous information, then denied AI use.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $55,597 attorney-fee sanction; published public reprimand; Bar referral; order-to-file-in-subsequent-cases for 12 months.

allegations.text: Attorney Joshua B. Watkins of Burrill Watkins LLC was publicly reprimanded, disqualified from the case, and sanctioned a combined $47,056.90 after forensic examination of his firm's ChatGPT Business Plan workspace confirmed hallucinated citations in filings. Counsel admitted generative-AI use and the court made an independent finding of false statements of law. The order was published in the Federal Supplement; the Bar was notified.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Non-existent federal case citations; genuine federal civil rights and landlord-tenant law
allegations.mitigation_gap: Attorney used ChatGPT to draft legal briefs and filed without verifying citations; forensic examination of the firm's ChatGPT workspace confirmed the AI origin.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $35,603.90 to Fite Defendants + $11,453 to Triad Defendants = $47,056.90; public reprimand; disqualification; Bar referral; order published.

allegations.text: Attorney Tricia S. Lindsay submitted memoranda containing fabricated citations and, belatedly, attributed them to LexisNexis's AI-driven features. The court rejected that attribution as "devoid of any specifics" and found only that Lindsay had used "an AI program that generated the fabricated citations." The court imposed a $2,500 Rule 11 sanction.
allegations.ai_system_name: Unspecified generative-AI program (court finding; LexisNexis attribution not credited)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Non-existent federal cases cited in memoranda of law; Fed. R. Civ. P. 11
allegations.mitigation_gap: Counsel submitted AI-generated citations without verification and offered a speculative, uncredited tool attribution after the fact.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $2,500 Rule 11 monetary sanction; fabricated citations submitted in federal court.

allegations.text: A second Rule 11 sanctions order in the Coomer v. Lindell/MyPillow litigation imposed a $5,000 sanction on attorney Christopher Kachouroff, who had earlier admitted to routinely using AI in drafting and whose prior brief contained approximately 30 defective or hallucinated AI-generated citations. The court also referred Kachouroff to the Colorado State Bar. Cumulative sanctions in the matter reached $8,000.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Accurate legal authority; Fed. R. Civ. P. 11
allegations.mitigation_gap: Counsel admitted routine AI use in drafting; failed to verify citations; second violation in same litigation.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $5,000 monetary sanction (second order); Bar referral; cumulative $8,000 in sanctions in this matter.

allegations.text: Defense counsel in a wage-and-hour class action admitted using LexisNexis's AI tool to help construct submissions without reviewing them for correctness, resulting in AI-hallucinated citations. The court imposed $1,710 in attorneys' fees (AI-authority-related) and a $1,000 monetary penalty on defense counsel under Rule 37, 28 U.S.C. § 1927, and inherent powers. A separate $46,980 discovery sanction was also imposed on defendants for unrelated witness-declaration misconduct.
allegations.deployer: Defense Counsel for Sushi Katsuei (unnamed in opinion)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine federal employment/wage-and-hour caselaw; court submission standards
allegations.mitigation_gap: Counsel used LexisNexis AI to draft court submissions without reviewing AI output for accuracy before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $1,710 AI-attributable attorneys' fees + $1,000 monetary penalty = $2,710 on defense counsel; fabricated citations submitted in class-action proceedings.

allegations.text: The Southern District of Indiana sanctioned attorney Tae Sture $2,000 under Rule 11 after his firm's paralegal used the Fastcase legal research tool, which generated hallucinated citations that were filed without verification. The court characterized the citations as "hallucinations generated by an artificial intelligence research tool" and specifically named Fastcase. The district judge reduced the Magistrate's recommended $7,500 sanction to $2,000.
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Genuine federal employment and civil-rights caselaw; Fed. R. Civ. P. 11
allegations.mitigation_gap: Paralegal used Fastcase AI without verification; supervising attorney failed to review citations before filing.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $2,000 Rule 11 sanction (reduced from $7,500 recommended); AI-hallucinated citations submitted in federal employment litigation.

allegations.text: The Seventh Circuit found that an immigration appeal brief was "riddled with AI hallucinations" including two dozen fabricated quotations and seven cases wrongly cited as circuit precedent. The brief was drafted by contracted attorney Farah Chalisa using ChatGPT, and filed by counsel of record Abdullah Salah. Salah was sanctioned $5,000 under Fed. R. App. P. 46(c); Chalisa was referred to the Illinois ARDC but not directly sanctioned because she had not appeared before the circuit.
allegations.deployer: Abdullah Salah (counsel of record); Farah Chalisa (contracted brief-drafter)
allegations.canonical_source_conflicted: Seventh Circuit immigration precedent; Fed. R. App. P. 46(c)
allegations.mitigation_gap: Contracted drafter used ChatGPT for copyediting without verification; counsel of record signed and filed without reviewing for AI hallucinations.
allegations.reliance_or_harm: $5,000 sanction on Salah; ARDC referral for Chalisa; court placed Salah on notice of potential disbarment; petition denied.
